content.full_text:
,4- 83-1'15" 
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
•• 
Administration 
•• 
Executive/legal 
(916) 322.5662 
32205660 
322·5901 
August 24, 1983 
Victor VanBourg 
VanBourg, Alle~Weinberg & Roger 
875 Battery Street 
San Francisco, CA 
94111 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
Statements of Economic ! nterest 
322-6444 
Re: 
Labor Council of Alameda County 
Dear Mr. VanBourg: 
On June 6, 1983, Steven Crooks, Legal Counsel in the Fair 
Political Practices Commission's Enforcement Division, wrote to 
you concerning the campaign disclosure filing obligations of the 
Labor Council of Alameda County. 
In that letter, Mr. Crooks 
cited the provisions of the Political Reform Act (Gov. Code Sec-
tions 81000-91014) and the Commission's regulations interpreting 
the campaign disclosure provisions of the Act (Title 2, Division 
6 of the California Administrative Code) as they apply to the 
activities of the Labor Council of Alameda County. 
The purposes of this letter are to further clarify how the 
campaign disclosure provisions apply to the activities of the 
Labor Council, and to set forth the disclosure obligations of 
the Labor Council. 
The facts as I understand them are that the Labor Council 
held a dinner on October 21, 1982, to raise money for communi-
cating with its members and urging them to vote for two candi-
dates for state constitutional offices in the 1982 General 
Election. 
Specifically, the proceeds from the sale of the 
tickets were used for the following purposes: 
(1) 
To produce and mail two expanded editions of the La-
bor Council's quarterly newsletter, each containing campaign 
advertisements and endorsements of candidates in the 1982 Gener-
al Election: and 
(2) 
To pay the costs of phone bank costs incurred when 
contacting union members for the purpose of urging them to vote 
for specific candidates on the General Election ballot. 


Victor VanBourg 
August 24, 1983 
Page 2 
The payments the Labor Council received from the sale of 
the dinner tickets meet the definition of "contributions" be-
cause the payments were "earmarked" for making contributions and 
expenditures for political purposes. 
That is, when the payments 
were made, the persons purchasing tickets knew that the payments 
were "earmarked" for political purposes. 
Commission regulation 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215(c) states that a payment is 
"earmarked" when: 
••• at the time of making the payment the donor 
knows or has reason to know that the payment or 
funds with which the payment will be commingled 
will be used to make contributions ••• or expendi-
tures (for poli tical purposes) •••• 
Upon receipt of $500 in contributions, the Labor Council 
met the definition of a "recipient committee" pursuant to Gov. 
Code Section 82013(a), and incurred an obligation to file a 
Statement of Organization with the Secretary of State. 
All contributions received (i.e., "earmarked" payments) , 
all payments made from those contributions, and all other expen-
ditures made for political purposes must be reported by a recip-
ient committee. 
We have determined that the payments made by the Labor 
Council in connection with the newsletters and the phone banks 
meet the definition of "expenditures" for political purposes un-
der Gov. Code Section 82025, and under 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18225(a) which provides that: 
An expenditure is any monetary or nonmonetary pay-
ment made for political purposes for which full 
and adequate consideration is not made to the 
donor. 
A payment is made for political purposes 
if it is: 
(1) 
For the purpose of influencing or 
attempting to influence the action of the voters 
for or against the nomination or election of a 
candidate or candidates, or the qualification or 
passage of any measure. 


Victor VanBourg 
August 24, 1983 
Page 3 
The payments made by the Labor Council for the phone banks 
and for the two special election editions of its newsletter 1/ 
meet this definition because the primary purpose of the 
-
communications was to urge the Council's members to vote for the 
two candidates. 
In its campaign disclosure statements, the Labor Council 
must report as contributions the total receipts from the sale of 
dinner tickets, and it must report all payments made in connec-
tion with the dinner, the phone banks and the two newsletters. 
Of course, if the Labor Council received any other 
contributions, or made any other expenditures for political 
purposes, they must also be reported. 
The following informational materials are enclosed, in-
cluding campaign disclosure forms for reporting activity which 
occurred during 1982, and a separate package of the same 
materials for activity which occurred during 1983: 
(1) 
The Political Reform Act of 1974; 
(2) 
Information Manual on Campaign Disclosure Provisions 
1/ 
The payments made by the Labor Council for the expanded 
editions of the newsletter do not meet the criteria set out in 2 
Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225 (b) (4) (C) which provides an exclu-
sion from the definition of "expenditure" for costs incurred for 
political purposes in connection with a regularly published 
newsletter whose circulation is limited to an organization's 
members, employees, or persons who request or purchase the 
publication. 
The payments do not meet these criteria because 
the exclusion applies only to the costs regularly incurred in 
publishing and distributing a newsletter. 
This subsection 
states that: 
•••• If additional costs are incurred because the 
newsletter or periodical is issued on other than 
its regular schedule, expanded in circulation, or 
substantially altered in style, size or format, 
the additional costs are expenditures. 
The payments in connection with the two newsletters were 
for additional costs because the newsletter was "substantially 
altered in style, size or format." 
Therefore, those payments 
are clearly "expenditures" for political purposes. 


Victor VanBourg 
August 24, 1983 
Page 4 
(3) 
Record Keeping Manual for Campaign Committees 
(4) 
Commission regulations: 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 
18215, 18225 and 18427 
(5) 
Statement of Organization (Form 410) and Recipient 
Committee Campaign Statements (Forms 420 and 450). 
I would be happy to discuss with you or the treasurer for 
the Labor Council's committee any specific questions about 
filling out the Statement of Organization and the required cam-
paign disclosure statements. 
Please call me at (916) 322-5662 
if you have any questions. 
enc. 
JP/bb 
Sincerely, 
V'

Political Reform Consultant 
Technical Assistance and 
Analysis Division

sections.conclusion: The payments the Labor Council received from the sale of dinner tickets meet the definition of 'contributions' because they were 'earmarked' for political purposes. Upon receipt of $500 in contributions, the Labor Council became a 'recipient committee' and must file a Statement of Organization with the Secretary of State and report all contributions received, all payments made from those contributions, and all other expenditures made for political purposes. The payments for phone banks and special editions of the newsletter constitute 'expenditures' for political purposes and must be reported in campaign disclosure statements.
sections.question_synthetic: What are the campaign disclosure filing obligations for the Labor Council of Alameda County, which held a fundraising dinner in October 1982 whose proceeds were used for political communications and member outreach urging support for two candidates?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that the Labor Council's dinner ticket sales constitute reportable contributions, that the Council became a recipient committee obligated to file with the Secretary of State, and that all expenditures for the phone banks and expanded newsletter editions must be reported as political expenditures.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Standard advice letter format with clear factual background and detailed conclusion. No explicit 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' section headers, but content is clearly structured as Q&A. Document includes extensive regulatory citations and footnote explaining newsletter cost exclusion criteria.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What are the campaign disclosure filing obligations for the Labor Council of Alameda County, which held a fundraising dinner in October 1982 whose proceeds were used for political communications and member outreach urging support for two candidates?

CONCLUSION: The payments the Labor Council received from the sale of dinner tickets meet the definition of 'contributions' because they were 'earmarked' for political purposes. Upon receipt of $500 in contributions, the Labor Council became a 'recipient committee' and must file a Statement of Organization with the Secretary of State and report all contributions received, all payments made from those contributions, and all other expenditures made for political purposes. The payments for phone banks and special editions of the newsletter constitute 'expenditures' for political purposes and must be reported in campaign disclosure statements.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises on campaign disclosure obligations for the Labor Council of Alameda County following its October 1982 fundraising dinner, clarifying that ticket sales are earmarked contributions, establishing recipient committee status, and requiring reporting of all related political expenditures.
